description:
You are assisting a script-developer who wants a quick, one-liner style utility that captures the *current* disk-usage of a specific directory tree.

    Goal
    ────
    1. Inspect every first-level sub-directory that already exists inside
       /home/user/datasets
       (do **NOT** recurse any deeper than one level).
    2. Produce a plain-text report file located at
       /home/user/reports/dataset_usage.log
       whose contents list the size of each of those first-level
       sub-directories.

    Exact output requirements for /home/user/reports/dataset_usage.log
    ------------------------------------------------------------------
    • One line per first-level sub-directory inside /home/user/datasets  
    • Each line must have exactly this format (no extra spaces):
          <directory_basename>|<size_in_KiB>
      where
        – <directory_basename> is just the name of the sub-directory,
          without any leading path components.
        – <size_in_KiB> is the first numeric field printed by
          “du -s -k <path>” (so it is expressed in kibibytes).
    • Sort the lines **alphabetically by the directory_basename**.
    • End the file with a single trailing newline.

    Example (illustrative only – real numbers may differ):
        images|2048
        texts|1024
        videos|5120

    Constraints & Hints
    -------------------
    • Accomplish the task with **one single terminal command** (multi-part
      shell pipelines joined by ‘|’, ‘>’, ‘;’, etc. still count as one
      command).
    • Do not hard-code any sizes; the command must query the live file-system.
    • Ensure /home/user/reports exists (create it if missing) and that
      /home/user/reports/dataset_usage.log is overwritten if it already
      exists.
    • No sudo/root privileges are available.

    Deliverable
    -----------
    After running the command, the only artefact the automated grader will
    inspect is the file:

        /home/user/reports/dataset_usage.log

    Make sure it satisfies all of the format rules above.
truth:
Pre-existing directories & files (mounted inside the grading
    container before the task starts):

    1. /home/user/datasets/
           ├── images/
           │     └── img.bin      (exactly 2 048 000 bytes)
           ├── texts/
           │     └── story.txt    (exactly 1 048 576 bytes)
           └── videos/
                 └── clip.mkv     (exactly 5 242 880 bytes)

       These sizes mean that a contemporary ext4 file-system will report,
       via “du -s -k”, something very close to:
           images  -> 2048
           texts   -> 1024
           videos  -> 5120
       (Values can vary by a few KiB on other file-systems, therefore the
       grader computes them dynamically; the numbers above must **NOT**
       be mentioned in the public task description.)

    2. /home/user/reports/   (may or may not exist; if present it is an
       empty directory, writable by the user).

    Grader verification logic (pseudo):
       expected_lines=$(du -sk /home/user/datasets/* |
                        awk '{ gsub(".*/","",$2); print $2"|" $1 }' |
                        sort)
       file_lines=$(cat /home/user/reports/dataset_usage.log | sort)
       if [ "$expected_lines" = "$file_lines" ]; then success else fail

    Therefore the presence, sorting, delimiters and sizes must all match
    exactly.
name: task_000000_8357d5ef
